response: Machine learning is a branch of AI that enables systems to learn from data and improve their performance without explicit programming.
Gradient descent is an iterative optimization algorithm that adjusts model parameters in the direction that minimizes the loss function.
A neural network is a computing system inspired by biological neurons, consisting of layers of interconnected nodes that process and transform data.
Backpropagation computes gradients by applying the chain rule from the output layer backward through each layer, enabling efficient weight updates.
Transformers are neural network architectures that use self-attention mechanisms to process all positions in a sequence simultaneously.
Overfitting occurs when a model learns the training data too well, including noise and outliers, resulting in poor performance on unseen data.
Transfer learning reuses a model trained on one task as the starting point for a different but related task, reducing training time and data requirements.
Batch normalization normalizes the input of each layer by adjusting and scaling the activations using the mean and variance computed over a mini-batch.
Dropout is a regularization technique that randomly sets a fraction of neuron activations to zero during training to prevent co-adaptation.
Attention computes a weighted sum of values based on the compatibility between a query and a set of keys, allowing the model to focus on relevant information.
Tokenization is the process of breaking text into smaller units called tokens, which can be words, subwords, or characters, for model processing.
The softmax function transforms a vector of real numbers into a probability distribution by exponentiating each element and normalizing by the sum.
Cross-entropy loss quantifies the difference between the predicted probability distribution and the true distribution, commonly used in classification.
Adam combines the benefits of momentum (tracking exponential moving average of gradients) and RMSprop (adaptive learning rates) for efficient optimization.
Data augmentation artificially expands training data by applying transformations like rotation, scaling, cropping, and color adjustments to existing samples.
Regularization adds constraints or penalties to the model to reduce complexity and prevent overfitting, with common methods including L1, L2, and dropout.
Embeddings are dense vector representations that map discrete items like words or categories into continuous vector spaces where similar items are nearby.
Beam search maintains the top-k most probable partial sequences at each step, expanding them until complete sequences are generated.
Perplexity is a measurement of how well a probability model predicts a sample, calculated as the exponential of the average negative log-likelihood.
Mixed precision training uses 16-bit floats for most computations and 32-bit for critical operations like loss scaling, reducing memory and increasing speed.
